name: Two-Step Peek
short: Exactly two committed steps, hand off mouse, preemptive stop.
source: Zasko — "In one call.. he learnt to fight like an IMMORTAL"
why: Two steps gets you to good lateral speed while the preemptive stop ensures you are at rest before the enemy enters your screen — turning the fight into a clean reaction shot rather than a scramble. The preemptive stop is the key: stopping before your crosshair reaches the enemy position guarantees you shoot from stillness, not mid-deceleration.
error: Micro-steps or jitter before the stop. Moving the mouse while strafing (blending motion and aim). Rushing through to 3+ steps.
explanation: Set up FAR from the wall — further than feels natural. Take EXACTLY two full, committed sideways steps using A or D only. Remove all fingers from movement keys BEFORE your crosshair reaches the enemy position. The crosshair must be idle during the two steps — completely still. Only micro-adjust AFTER full stop. Verbalize the angle before every rep: say "I'm set up for logs / catwalk / top box" out loud. This forces deliberate intent rather than mindless repetition.
cue: "Two steps, lift, wait." Verbalize your target angle before each rep.
condition: 10 consecutive angles with zero mouse movement during the step phase. On VOD replay, the opponent should see instant death with no warning.
location: Custom game, any map — showers / catwalk / B main

name: Idle Crosshair (Hand-Off-Mouse) Drill
short: Walk to angle, physically remove hand from mouse, peek, replace.
source: Zasko — "You're Making Gunfights WAY Harder Than They Should Be" / "In one call.."
why: Blending mouse movement with strafing creates a curved, unpredictable crosshair path that misses most of the time. Your brain defaults to this habit under pressure. Physically lifting the hand breaks the neural loop completely, forcing the crosshair to land based on your pre-aim position alone — which is how Radiant-level peeking actually works.
error: Dragging crosshair while moving. Multiple micro-adjustments. Fidgeting after stop.
explanation: Walk up to an angle in custom game. Place crosshair at head height pointing into the wall. Physically lift your mouse hand off the mouse completely. Take your two steps. Peek. Place hand back on mouse ONLY AFTER the peek is complete. This sounds extreme — that's the point. It eliminates the blend of motion and aim that plagues 90% of players. The hand-off forces you to rely on positioning and pre-aim rather than reflexive mouse dragging. Do 15+ reps this way. Then reintroduce the mouse gently for micro-correction only.
cue: "Hand off the mouse until stopped."
condition: You need at most ONE small mouse movement per kill. If you're making 3+ corrections per fight, restart the rep.

name: Slice & Fall Back
short: Peek close to wall, gather info, fall back instantly — zero commitment.
source: Zasko — "Get-Out-Of-Jail-Free Slice" / "How To ACTUALLY Move In Valorant"
why: Committing to a wide swing without information is statistically bad: if two enemies are holding, you die. The slice gives you a free data point with near-zero risk because staying close to the wall means you're exposed for only a fraction of a second. Think of it as a Jett dash you can do with any agent — you see enemy position, then fall back without taking damage.
error: Standing too far from the wall during the slice. Not pre-stopping before clearing. Committing to a full wide swing on first peek.
explanation: The slice is your information peek — not your kill peek. Stand CLOSE to the wall. Preemptively stop BEFORE your crosshair clears the corner. This gives you the mini-Jett-dash effect: if 3 enemies are holding, you fall back with zero damage taken. Only commit a wide swing after you confirm: (1) there is one enemy, and (2) their crosshair has been baited tight by your slice. Drill: slice → info → duck back → wide swing with broken enemy crosshair. Practice on one angle for 10+ reps until fallback is reflexive.
cue: "Close to wall, stop early, gather only."
condition: 5 consecutive angles where you fall back cleanly without taking damage on the slice.

name: Slice → Wide Swing Combo
short: Open with slice (info + condition), follow with wide swing (kill).
source: Zasko — "you're killing your own aim" / "if you keep peeking like this, you will NOT rank up"
why: The slice forces the enemy to commit their crosshair tight to where you appeared. When you then wide swing, their crosshair is displaced — they must flick to re-acquire you. This buys you 100–200ms of free aim time. Radiants don't go straight to the wide swing because they understand it removes the information and conditioning advantage. The combo converts a 50/50 duel into a structured kill.
error: Going straight to wide swing with zero info. Re-peeking same way twice. Rushing the wide swing before enemy is conditioned.
explanation: Combo A: slice close → enemy expects re-slice → wide swing out (their crosshair is tight, yours is wide). Combo B: slice → miss → wait 1 second → wide swing (they expected a re-slice, you broke their pattern). Combo C: slice → kill → wide swing quickly (second enemy expects static hold, you go wide). Wide swing technique: face against wall → explode out with conviction → have crosshair on head BEFORE stopping → stop → shoot within 10ms max. Practice on one angle with a friend or custom bots. Drill each combo 10 reps before moving to the next.
cue: "Slice to condition. Wide to kill. Never skip the setup."
condition: 10 wide swings where crosshair is on head BEFORE the stop — zero flicks after stopping.
location: Custom game with friend, or bots

name: Sage Wall Peek Drill
short: Exactly 1–2 shadow steps from a Sage wall — trains silent, precise exposure.
source: w0rthyTV — "Movement & Peeking Mastery, How To Fix It"
why: The Sage wall acts as a hard physical constraint: it prevents you from over-exposing while giving you a surface to practice precise step counts. 1–2 steps keeps you in the shadow zone — direction change gives 100% accuracy, and short bursts stay below the footstep audio threshold. The wall enforces this envelope without relying on your own self-discipline.
error: More than 2 steps (shooting error goes blue). Diagonal movement (W+A or W+D). Standing still after shot.
explanation: In the practice range, erect a Sage wall. Position yourself behind it with bots spread in front. Peek in and out using exactly 1–2 steps per direction — no more. Short bursts like this stay below the footstep audio threshold and return to zero velocity at each direction change, giving full accuracy. Start with the rightmost bot and work progressively left, forcing tighter and tighter angle exposures. Enable the shooting error indicator — lines must stay yellow, never blue. Upgrade: add pre-fire movement (switch direction keys going back INTO wall while maintaining aim line).
cue: "One, two, out — one, two, back."
condition: 5 full passes across all bots with shooting error staying yellow every single time.

name: Teammate Angle Drill
short: Friend holds common angles statically. You practice peeking with correct timing.
source: w0rthyTV — "You Move Like A Bot (Movement Guide)"
why: Range bots don't have reaction times and never punish bad technique — they only test aim. A human defender forces you to win through movement timing and angle mechanics, not just raw mechanical skill. If you're winning by out-aiming instead of out-moving, the drill isn't teaching what you need.
error: Using diagonal movement (W+A or W+D). Jiggling instead of committing. Winning by aim instead of movement timing.
explanation: Have a friend hold a common angle without repositioning. You practice peeking it with correct technique: straight-line single-key movement, pause-peek entry, shadow steps, correct head-height pre-aim. Friend fires as fast as possible — they are the punishment for bad form. Your goal is not to win by out-aiming; it's to win because your movement timing and technique make you harder to hit. Progress: if you win 60%+ of engagements, move to harder angles or have the friend play off-angles to expose bad crosshair placement habits.
cue: "Straight line, not diagonal. Technique wins, not aim."
condition: Win 60%+ of engagements through technique (not out-aiming) across 20-30 reps per angle.
location: Custom game, any map — 3-5 common angles per session

name: Pause-Peek (Priming the Angle)
short: Run up to angle, brief prime stop, then swing. Used by TenZ, Florescent.
source: w0rthyTV — "How To Hit CLEAN One Taps"
why: Running at full speed into an angle requires ~2 seconds of deceleration before you can shoot accurately. Pause-peeking eliminates that delay by briefly stopping at the entry point — you are already at near-zero velocity when you swing, so your first bullet is accurate from frame one. The enemy still sees a fast peek because you built momentum before the pause.
error: Running straight into open angle with no pause (shooting error terrible). Exposing more than half your body. Stopping too far back from the entry point (too slow).
explanation: Running full speed then stopping takes ~2 seconds before accurate shooting. The pause-peek solves this: run up → brief stop at the entry point → swing out. Because you pause at the entry point rather than at the start of your approach, you're shooting from near-stillness with maximum first-shot accuracy, while momentum from the run keeps your overall peek speed high. Drill: on a common angle (A main, catwalk, B main), run up to the entry line, pause-peek, shoot, return. Aim for half-body or less exposed. Repeat 10-15 reps per angle. Watch the shooting error indicator — must go yellow on your swing.
cue: "Run up. Here. Out." (3-beat rhythm)
condition: Pause is visible in VOD review. Shooting error shows yellow on swing. Half-body or less exposed per rep.
location: Custom game, common map angles (catwalk, A main, B main)

name: 5-Step 45-Min Aim Warm-Up
short: Progressive range warm-up from static flicks to cover peeking.
source: w0rthyTV — "Best Way To Warm Up / Improve Aim In VALORANT"
why: Motor skills warm up in layers: fine motor control (finger precision) needs to activate before gross motor (full arm movement), and both before reactive tasks (live DM). Skipping straight to DM is like sprinting before stretching — you're practicing with degraded hardware.
error: Rushing through steps. Moving to medium/hard before accuracy is solid at current level. Skipping the 25-perfect-burst milestone.
explanation:
Total: 45 minutes. Only advance Easy → Medium → Hard when accuracy is solid at the current level.

Step 1 (Easy bots, standing still): slow flicks to bot heads, 1 bullet each — feel the sensitivity, establish the head connection.

Step 2 (Wall burst drill): stop-strafe into a wall (hold A → release → press D = full stop), fire a precise burst. Must achieve 25 PERFECT bursts in a row — reset count if any burst is imperfect. This milestone forces the nervous system to confirm the pattern.

Step 3 (Counter-strafe into bots, Easy): apply step 2 movement to live bots. Stop-strafe → shoot heads. Repeat until comfortable.

Step 4 (Peek from cover): peek straight out from behind wall, shoot head, return. Close range first.

Step 5 (Distance variation): same as step 4 but vary target distance — feel how accuracy changes (close = burst/tap fine; far = tap only).
cue: "Accuracy first. Speed comes on its own."
condition: Complete all 5 steps through Hard mode within 45 minutes with consistent accuracy at each level.

name: Ping Tracking Drill
short: Ping wall at head level, burst/tap onto it while moving — never drop below.
source: w0rthyTV — "THE AIM Mistake You Don't Know About"
why: Most players unconsciously drag the mouse down during bursts — a habit from tracking body shots rather than head shots. This creates the "U-shape" pattern where the crosshair drops below the head after shot 2 or 3. The ping gives an external reference point that reveals this habit instantly, because any U-shape is visually obvious against a static marker.
error: Crosshair drops below the ping (U-shape error). Pulling down on mouse during single taps. Not waiting for gun reset between bursts.
explanation:
Place a ping on a wall at head level in the range or custom game. Move side to side, burst/tap fire onto each ping — NEVER drop below it.

Tap mode: one bullet per ping. Burst mode: 2-3 bullets, full stop, wait for gun animation to reset (crosshair returns to first-bullet position), then burst again.

If crosshair drops below the ping → U-shape problem identified → restart, keep higher. This trains you to stop the pull-down reflex that most players apply even to single-tap shots.
cue: "Stay on the ping. Never go below."
condition: Zero crosshair drops below the ping across 50 taps or 20 bursts in a full session.

name: Yoru Clone Head-Level Study
short: Place Yoru clone at each angle to see exact head level. Memorize visually.
source: w0rthyTV — "THE AIM Mistake You Don't Know About"
why: Head level is not constant — it changes based on terrain height, distance, and angle geometry. Guessing head level even by 5 pixels means your first shot misses without you understanding why. The Yoru clone gives a pixel-accurate reference that you then commit to muscle memory through repeated visualization.
error: Guessing head level instead of checking. Checking once and forgetting — the point is memorization. Only doing it on maps you already play well.
explanation:
In custom game with Yoru selected: place the clone at an angle you struggle with. The clone's head shows the exact pixel to pre-aim for that specific angle at that specific distance.

Memorize: "Head level for this angle is HERE." Repeat for 5-10 commonly fought angles per session. Focus on your 3-map pool — not every map.

Over time, your crosshair placement becomes muscle memory rather than guesswork. This directly addresses the U-shape problem by teaching you where head level actually is before you start the fight.
cue: "Where's the head level here? Lock it in."
condition: Can pre-place crosshair without conscious thought at memorized angles in ranked. Test yourself: approach a known angle in ranked and note whether you pre-aimed it correctly.

name: Deadzone Stop Timing
short: Shoot when character speed drops to the accuracy threshold — not before.
source: w0rthyTV / n0ted — "Counter-Strafe Matters" / Fundamenty research
why: Valorant's accuracy is tied directly to movement speed. While moving at full speed, bullet spread can be 3-4x wider than standing still. Accuracy returns once velocity drops below roughly 30% of max speed — you don't need to fully stop. Counter-strafing reaches zero velocity faster than releasing alone, but Valorant decelerates quickly regardless, so the time saved is small. The shooting error graph makes this tangible: shoot when it's yellow, not before.
error: Shooting while still moving (blue error lines). Waiting too long after stop and losing the timing window. Jitter from over-counter-strafing.
explanation:
Enable the shooting error graph in the range (Settings → Video → Show Bullet Impacts and Shooting Error). Strafe A, then either release or press D (counter-strafe), shoot ONLY when lines are yellow.

For the Sheriff drill (n0ted method): strafe exactly 1 unit, immediately press counter-strafe key + click simultaneously. If timing is correct, accuracy lines cluster tightly around your shot.

Use whichever stop technique (release vs. counter-strafe) keeps your crosshair most stable — that's the correct method for your hardware and sensitivity. Progress to 2-unit strafes only after 1-unit is fully consistent.
cue: "Release — wait for yellow — click."
condition: Guardian DM: >60% first-bullet headshots in a 5-minute session. Sheriff drill: 10 consecutive 1-unit strafe shots hit before progressing.
location: Range (error graph on), then Guardian DM or Sheriff in-game

name: Tension Fizzle (Explosive Launch + Float)
short: Flick starts with high tension. On arrival, physically release grip to "float".
source: Zasko — "3 Reasons You Keep Whiffing" / "why calm aim is the biggest lie"
why: High grip tension during a flick is correct for the launch phase — it generates speed. But maintaining that tension on arrival causes the mouse to overshoot because your grip is still pushing. Releasing grip as you arrive converts from "power mode" to "precision mode" mid-flick. This is the actual mechanism behind what pros describe as "floaty" or "calm" aim. IMPORTANT: Tension Fizzle is for situations where a flick is your only option — crosshair far from target, no time to MASS-position. It does NOT replace MASS Protocol. Train MASS first.
error: Maintaining high grip tension through the entire flick. Over-flicking past the target. Holding tight near target = overflick oscillation.
explanation:
Every flick has two phases:

Phase 1 (launch): explosive, directed — high grip tension is correct here. Generate speed.

Phase 2 (arrival): as crosshair approaches the target, physically release grip and let the mouse "float." Holding high tension through arrival = overflick.

The "calm" or "floaty" aim pros describe is this tension release, not relaxation throughout. Practice in Kovaak "Adjust Track" or "Aim Chainer" — when the target changes direction, resist the hard-flick impulse. Grip tight → release. Grip tight → release. Repeat until the release becomes automatic on target arrival.
cue: "Explosive launch. Float on arrival."
condition: No hard overflick when a target changes direction unexpectedly across 10 consecutive reps.
location: Kovaak (Adjust Track / Aim Chainer), then Valorant range strafing bots

name: n0ted 3-Exercise Kovaak Warmup
short: Micro-adjust → flick prediction → strafe tracking. 30-40 min in Kovaak.
source: n0ted — "Radiant University EP.2 N0ted's Warmup"
why: The three exercises target three different neural subsystems: micro-adjustments train fine motor precision, flick prediction trains temporal anticipation (where the target will be), and strafe tracking trains your ability to use your own body movement as an aiming tool. Doing all three before ranked primes every relevant subsystem rather than just one.
error: Skipping exercise 1 and rushing to harder content. Chasing score instead of rep quality. Not micro-correcting after overflicks — the correction IS the training.
explanation:
Playlist: search "KovaaKsBottingJumboBattlepass" in Kovaak online playlists. Three exercises, 10-20 min each.

Exercise 1 (Micro-Adjust Horizontal Lines): slow start, side-to-side along horizontal line, aim HIGHER than default, increase speed gradually. After every overflick — micro-correct. The correction is the rep, not the hit. Cue: "Flick then micro-correct."

Exercise 2 (Flick Prediction on Bot Spawn): stay at back of range, wait and listen for bot audio cue, flick to where you PREDICT the bot will be — perfect horizontal line to head level, micro-adjust if overshot. Use Sheriff (no spray) for accountability. Cue: "First flick correct, or micro-correct. Two acceptable outcomes."

Exercise 3 (Strafe Tracking with Edge Technique): strafe left-right while tracking bot head. Place crosshair on the OPPOSITE edge of head from your strafe direction — movement brings crosshair onto head naturally. Cue: "Edge of head, opposite side of your movement."
cue: "Micro-correct every miss. The correction IS the rep."
condition: Can apply the same physical feeling in Valorant range immediately after the session — describe it in one word (e.g., 'snappy', 'floaty', 'grounded'). Aim for ~80% accuracy per exercise.

name: Sheriff Counter-Strafe Drill
short: 1-unit strafe, counter-strafe + shoot simultaneously. No forgiveness.
source: n0ted — "Counter-Strafe Matters.. MORE THAN YOU THINK"
why: The Sheriff exposes every timing error because it has zero forgiveness: a shot 20ms early is a full miss. When you can hit consistent 1-unit strafe shots with the Sheriff, the same timing with a Vandal feels trivially easy — same principle as training with a heavier weight so the game weight feels light. The Sheriff trains your nervous system to fire at the exact moment of velocity zero.
error: Shooting before counter-strafe is registered. Strafing more than 1 unit (too much deceleration time). Using Vandal/Phantom as a crutch when Sheriff timing fails.
explanation:
Setup: Valorant range, 100 bots on strafe, equip Sheriff.

Step 1: Strafe exactly 1 unit (press D).
Step 2: Immediately press A (counter-strafe) + click at exactly the same time as pressing A.
Step 3: If timing is correct, accuracy lines cluster tightly around your shot.

Rhythm: D press → A press + shoot → D press → A press + shoot. Progress to 2-unit strafes only after 1-unit is fully consistent. If you can hit this drill with the Sheriff, Vandal and Phantom will feel forgiving by comparison.
cue: "Press A + click. Same input. Same time."
condition: 10 consecutive 1-unit strafe shots with the Sheriff before progressing to 2-unit.
location: Valorant practice range (shooting error graph on)

name: Strafe-and-Shoot Timing Drill
short: Fire 1–2 bullets at each direction change. A→D window. Never spray.
source: n0ted — "*No Bs* How to STRAFE & SHOOT n0ted style"
why: Continuous movement while shooting seems impossible because most players try to shoot between direction changes. n0ted's method fires at the direction change — precisely because that's the moment of minimum velocity. The enemy sees continuous movement but bullets are hitting. This is a technical application of Valorant's movement accuracy curve that creates a real skill gap against players trying to full-stop. NOTE: This is the same accuracy window as Deadzone Stop, packaged inside strafe motion. Learn Deadzone Stop first, then apply it here.
error: Firing while still building to max speed. Pressing W+D or W+A (stacks W-velocity, raises movement error). Spraying more than 2 bullets per window. Lifting fingers from keyboard.
explanation:
Build to max speed before engaging (strafe A or D only — never W+A or W+D). At the direction change (A→D or D→A): fire 1-2 bullets within the direction-change window. This is the dead zone — minimum velocity, maximum accuracy.

Never lift fingers from keyboard — transition from A to D is a simultaneous press, not a lift-and-move.

Phantom: faster fire rate (11 rps), more bullets land in the accuracy window. Vandal: slower fire rate (9.75 rps), tighter first-shot tolerance. Train both.

After shooting, continue strafing — never hold still.

Practice: 10 min in range, then apply in Ghost DM.
cue: "D to A is where the bullet goes straight. Shoot at the change."
condition: In Ghost DM: all kills via 1-2 shot bursts at direction changes — zero standing-still shots. Can sustain for a full 10-minute DM session.

name: Uncomfortable Position Flick Drill
short: After kill, stay in that crosshair position. Next rep is from there.
source: n0ted — "Radiant University EP.3 The TRUTH on Why YOU Keep WHIFFING"
why: Ranked games never give you comfortable starting positions — enemies appear when your crosshair is already displaced from clearing the last angle. Standard aim training always resets to center, so your "comfortable" flick is a skill that only exists in training. By removing the reset, you build the exact skill the game actually demands: accurate flicks from unpredictable starting angles.
error: Resetting crosshair to center after each kill. Only practicing close-range, comfortable positions. Chasing score instead of angle variety.
explanation:
After killing a bot, do NOT re-center your crosshair. Stay exactly where it landed. When the next bot spawns, flick from that uncomfortable angle.

Phase 1 (Wrist Domination): pick a physical reference (edge of mousepad), flick as fast as possible for 180° sweeps — full wrist motion, don't care about misses, let wrist teach itself.

Phase 2 (Straight Lines): after wrist exercise, transition to precise straight horizontal lines, full wrist, no rushing.

Phase 3 (Uncomfortable positions): the main drill — follow bot spawn without any center-reset.
cue: "The game won't let you reset. Practice shouldn't either."
condition: 15+ reps at varied crosshair positions without a single center-reset. Every rep starts from where the last one ended.
location: Kovaak — any aim scenario, or Valorant range

name: Target Transitions (Delusional Trust)
short: Assume T1 is dead. Snap to T2 with zero delay — before kill animation.
source: Zasko — "Mada Makes Fragging EFFORTLESS"
why: Waiting 150–200ms for the kill animation before engaging the next target is one of the most measurable performance gaps between high-Immortal and Radiant. Mada's "delusional trust" flips the logic: assume the first shot worked, move on instantly. Even if you missed, you're now in a better position for target 2. The 150ms saved on every multi-kill compounds across every round.
error: Waiting 150–200ms to visually confirm the first kill before switching. Watching the death animation play before moving eyes to T2.
explanation:
When facing multiple enemies: shoot T1 → ASSUME dead → snap to T2 immediately. Do not wait for kill animation or audio cue.

Practice in custom: face two dummy positions. Fire one shot at the first → immediately flick to the second. Your crosshair should reach T2 before T1's death animation plays.

Build the reflex: fire → move → evaluate, not fire → evaluate → move.
cue: "Fire and forget. Next target immediately."
condition: 3 double-kills in DM within 5 minutes where your crosshair reaches T2 before T1's death animation plays.

name: MASS Protocol DM
short: Every kill: Move → Aim → Stand Still → Shoot. No exceptions.
source: w0rthyTV — "How To Aim Like Florescent" / "Why Your Movement Sucks Not Your Aim"
why: Analysis of Florescent's play showed the crosshair was pre-positioned on heads through movement — almost no flicking required. Flicking is the symptom of arriving somewhere without your crosshair already prepared. MASS Protocol retrains the order of operations so movement does 90% of the aiming work and the remaining 10% is a tiny micro-adjustment, not a screen-wide flick. MASS = how you position before contact. Tension Fizzle = what you do when a flick is the only option left. Learn MASS first.
error: Flick kills (crosshair was not pre-set by movement). Standing still and shooting. Winning the fight through aim instead of movement-based crosshair placement.
explanation:
MASS = Move, Aim, Stand Still, Shoot.

Move: use WASD to enter the fight, create the angle, get into position — movement IS the aim.
Aim: small crosshair adjustment at relative head level.
Stand Still: stop momentum before firing (shadow step or full stop).
Shoot: tap or burst, not spray.

Practice full DM sessions where every kill follows MASS. Any flick kill = the rep doesn't count — note it and replay the angle with correct form.
cue: "Move first. Never flick. Movement places the crosshair."
condition: Full DM session (10-15 min) where every kill is Move→Aim→Stop→Shoot. Count flick kills — target is zero per session.

name: Odin Head-Height Calibration
short: Fire Odin burst at angle, run back, pre-aim the bullet holes — your exact head level.
source: Zasko — "3 Simple Ways To Improve Your Crosshair Placement"
why: Most crosshair placement errors are imprecise head-level guesses that drift 10–20 pixels high or low depending on terrain. The Odin leaves a visible hole pattern at the exact height your crosshair was. Running back and pre-aiming that hole teaches you the correct absolute height for that angle through kinesthetic memory rather than abstract visual estimation.
error: Doing this once and not repeating for different terrains. Using auto-fire instead of a controlled burst. Checking the holes from the wrong distance.
explanation:
Setup: Custom game, any map, equip Odin (high ammo, clear wall marks).

Step 1: Walk to the exact peek position you struggle with in ranked.
Step 2: Aim at the wall in the direction enemies would be.
Step 3: Fire a controlled 3-5 bullet burst at your best guess for head height.
Step 4: Run back to your normal approach distance.
Step 5: Pre-aim the bullet hole you left — that is the calibrated head level for this angle.

Repeat 5x per session on your 3-5 hardest angles. In your next ranked game: recall the bullet-hole position before peeking that angle.
cue: "Where did the holes land? Pre-aim that spot."
condition: Can pre-aim 5 memorized angles within 1 second of approach without hesitation in ranked.

name: Kovaak → Game Transfer Protocol
short: Kovaak feel → range → custom → DM. Copy-paste the sensation at each step.
source: Zasko — "Fixing his SHAKY AIM in just 30 minutes"
why: Kovaak builds isolated skill in an artificial environment. The transfer problem — where players can't replicate Kovaak performance in ranked — happens because they fail to bridge the contexts. Each step in this protocol reduces the novelty gap: range is closer to real movement, custom removes DM chaos, DM adds enemies. The goal is to carry the exact physical sensation from step to step, not the score.
error: Jumping straight from Kovaak to ranked with no bridge step. Treating each step as a separate activity instead of continuously transferring the same feeling.
explanation:
Step 1 (Kovaak): pick ONE scenario type (flicking, tracking, or evasive). Run 10-15 min in free play. Focus on the physical sensation — how does your hand feel when doing it correctly? Name it: 'floaty,' 'snappy,' 'grounded.'

Step 2 (Valorant range): immediately open range and replicate that same feeling on bots. Do NOT adjust sensitivity or grip — copy the feeling, not the score.

Step 3 (Custom game): play solo and find one fight where that feeling shows up naturally. Note the exact moment it clicks.

Step 4 (DM): enter DM carrying that feeling. First 2 minutes = "feeling check" — am I doing it right?

Step 5 (Ranked): the feeling is the one cue you bring in.
cue: "Copy the feeling, not the score. Feel it in each step."
condition: Can name in one word the physical sensation of correct aim (e.g. 'snappy'). Can immediately identify in a DM session whether you have it or not.

name: Anti-Mirror Dodge
short: After shooting, strafe OPPOSITE to enemy movement. Never mirror.
source: Zasko — "why you're getting destroyed when angle holding" / n0ted
why: When you mirror the enemy's strafe direction, your relative motion appears zero — you look static to their aim. Strafing opposite forces them to stop their tracking motion and reverse direction, adding a minimum 100–150ms cognitive delay. This is how you make yourself hard to hit without doing anything mechanically complex.
error: Standing still after missing. Mirroring the enemy's strafe direction (gives them a static target). Waiting for visual kill confirmation before moving.
explanation:
After shooting and missing (or after any shot): do NOT mirror the enemy's strafe. If they went right and you go right — mirroring, predictable. Go opposite (anti-mirror). This forces the enemy to recalibrate their aim track, resets their reaction time advantage, and keeps you evasive.

Your click must be INSTANTLY followed by a movement key — 0ms delay. Practice in DM: after every shot, immediately press a direction key opposite to enemy movement. No looking at kill feed. No hesitation.
cue: "Click — move opposite — reset."
condition: 5 consecutive duels in DM where every shot is followed by immediate lateral movement, opposite to enemy direction.

name: Shoot-Then-Move (Zero Gap)
short: Every shot instantly triggers a movement key. Zero evaluation gap.
source: Zasko — "How To ACTUALLY Burst Like PRIMMIE" / "How To ACTUALLY Move In Valorant"
why: The 200ms post-shot pause is a deeply wired habit from FPS games where kill confirmation matters (economy, looting). In Valorant, that pause is free time for the enemy to trade you. Programming the "shoot = move" reflex automatically positions you out of any pre-aimed crosshair before the enemy can react. You become permanently dynamic — never a static target.
error: Pausing after shot to check hit. Moving then stopping then moving (the 200ms linger habit). Shooting more than 4 bullets in a burst before moving.
explanation:
The rule: fire, then INSTANTLY press a movement key. Not "fire, check, then move." Not "fire, see miss, then move." Always. Even if you got the kill — move.

For precision: one shot → press D or A immediately (same frame as click).
For bursting: burst 2-4 bullets → immediately strafe to cover. Never spray more than 4 before moving — inaccuracy spikes after shot 4.

Practice: in the Valorant range, fire at a bot → press D immediately → fire again → press A immediately. The motion is fire+move as one fluid impulse.

In DM: every single shot triggers immediate movement. Apply anti-mirror: move opposite to enemy strafe direction. If cover is available, retreat to it after firing. If not, anti-mirror dodge.
cue: "Shoot is the trigger for move. Same frame. No checking."
condition: Full DM session (10-15 min) where you never stand still for 2+ frames after any shot. Zero post-shot pauses. Count violations.

name: Deliberate DM (Gym Reps Protocol)
short: Treat every DM peek like a ranked angle. 1-second pause. Far back. 1v1 only.
source: Zasko — "before you queue, PLEASE do this" / w0rthyTV Measured Fights Protocol
why: Mindless DM reinforces whatever habits you already have — including the bad ones. The 1-second pause before each angle is deliberate practice: it forces your brain into conscious mode where a new pattern can be installed. Automaticity comes after the pattern is installed, not before.
error: Running around mindlessly. Fighting while exposed to multiple angles simultaneously. Ignoring kills that were wrong reps (flukes or flicks). Chasing score.
explanation:
Treat DM like structured gym reps — slow, deliberate, form-perfect angle clearing.

Rules:
1. Tiny 1-second pause before every angle — believe someone is there before peeking.
2. Set up far back from each angle — expose yourself to ONE enemy at a time.
3. Peek → micro-adjust → shoot — never move to next angle mid-fight.
4. Ignore getting shot in the back — reset and maintain perfect form.
5. Wrong reps don't count — if you won via a flick or standing still, note it and replay the angle with correct form.

Zasko's session structure: Pre-DM visualizer (watch 2-5 min of coaching DM footage) → warm-up DMs → 3 structured post-comp DMs with deliberate form.

Note: MASS Protocol and Sheriff drills happen inside this structure — Deliberate DM is the framework, not a separate technique.
cue: Every angle: 1-second pause, believe someone's there, then peek.
condition: Can send a 5-min DM recording where every single peek has a visible pause and far-back setup. Zero rushed angles.

name: DM Minimap Respawn Habit
short: Every respawn with shield: freeze, read minimap, identify 2+ red dots before moving.
source: w0rthyTV — "How Radiants Look At The Radar/Minimap"
why: Radiants check the minimap at specific game states as a reflex — post-respawn, post-cover, post-kill. The DM spawn shield is a controlled, pressure-free window to practice the exact minimap-first reflex without ranking consequences. After 20+ sessions of this habit, you automatically check minimap at these moments in ranked without conscious thought.
error: Immediately running after respawn. Never checking minimap. Entering fights without knowing enemy positions.
explanation:
Every time you respawn in DM with the spawn shield active, DO NOT move immediately. Use the shield time to:
1. Look at the minimap.
2. Identify all visible red dots.
3. Plan your next engagement before moving.

This trains the Passive → Active mode transition at every life. Build the reflex: respawn → minimap → hunt.

After a full session with this habit, you'll automatically check minimap in ranked at these trigger moments: post-spawn, post-kill, post-cover.
cue: "Respawn → minimap → hunt."
condition: Can name 2+ enemy positions from minimap before engaging on every single respawn during a DM session. Test yourself: after each death in ranked, note whether you knew where enemies were before pushing.

name: Breath Before Swing
short: One deep exhale before every intentional peek. Resets arousal/tension state.
source: w0rthyTV / Fundamenty — diaphragmatic regulation + Sheriff DM practice
why: Elevated fear or arousal causes the muscles around your wrist and fingers to tighten involuntarily, degrading fine motor precision. The culprit is the arousal/tension state itself — not heart rate alone. A single slow exhale activates the parasympathetic nervous system and reduces involuntary grip tension. The primary in-game value is resetting that tension state before you peek, not dramatically lowering your resting heart rate.
error: Peeking immediately without the breath (arousal-driven grip tension causes jitter and overflick). Skipping the exhale on "important" rounds where you need it most.
explanation:
Before every intentional peek (not reactive fights): take one slow diaphragmatic exhale — 4-6 seconds. This activates the parasympathetic system and produces a transient reduction in grip tension. The primary benefit is the tension reset, not a dramatic heart rate change from a single breath.

Practice in Sheriff DM: take a breath before every single engagement for one full session. The Sheriff's zero-forgiveness makes composure immediately measurable — you will feel the difference between peeking tense vs. peeking relaxed.

Build this as the last thing you do in buy phase before moving out.
cue: "Exhale — then peek."
condition: Full Sheriff DM session (10 min) with one breath before every fight. Measurable: compare your accuracy on breaths vs. no-breath rounds.

name: Green Mode Lock (One Cue Rule)
short: Before ranked: choose exactly ONE cue. Apply it round 1. Then forget mechanics.
source: Zasko — "This Subtle Mistake WASTES So Much Time.." / Green/Pink Mindset
why: Motor skills (aiming, movement) are executed by your cerebellum and basal ganglia — not your prefrontal cortex. Consciously analyzing aim technique mid-gunfight routes processing through the wrong brain region, which is always slower and degraded compared to automatic execution. Green mode is deliberately suppressing the analytical system so the faster trained motor system can operate unimpeded.
error: Bringing analytical (Pink) thinking into ranked games. Thinking about crosshair position mid-gunfight. Trying to apply 3+ cues at once.
explanation:
Green mindset = pure performance mode. Flow state, intuition, no conscious thought about mechanics. Pink mindset = analytical — for drills, VOD review, and range only. NEVER Pink during ranked.

Pre-queue protocol:
1. Choose exactly ONE cue from your current drill.
2. Apply it consciously in round 1.
3. Then go Green — play chess, not mechanics.

Between rounds: mute game sound, breathe deep, loosen shoulders, release mouse tension. Reset to Green.

If you catch yourself thinking "where is my crosshair?" during a gunfight — you are in Pink during Green time. Notice it, let it go, return to trusting your body.
cue: "One cue before queue. Then forget mechanics — play chess."
condition: 3 ranked games without consciously analyzing aim technique mid-gunfight. Track in session log: did you stay Green or slip into Pink?

name: 5-Why Post-Death Review
short: After a confusing death: ask "why?" five times to reach the root cause.
source: Zasko — "You'll Never Rank Up Until You Fix This"
why: The first answer to "why did I die?" is almost always surface-level ("I missed") and addresses only the symptom. Surface-level analysis leads to surface-level training decisions. The 5-Why technique consistently reaches the underlying structural problem within 5 layers. In Valorant, this transforms "I missed" into actionable insights like "I don't pre-visualize before pushing B main."
error: Stopping at "I missed" or "I peeked wrong." The surface answer is never the root cause.
explanation:
After each death in VOD review: ask "why?" five times.

Example:
Why did I die? → I missed the shot.
Why did I miss? → I was moving when I fired.
Why was I moving? → I panicked after hearing footsteps.
Why did I panic? → I didn't pre-visualize the scenario.
Why didn't I pre-visualize? → I wasn't in the right mental state before peeking.

Root cause: missing pre-peek mental preparation, not aim. This transforms review from blame ("I missed") into insight ("I need to pre-visualize before pushing B.").

One death, five whys, one written insight per session. Over 5 sessions, patterns will emerge.
cue: "Why? Why? Why? Why? Why? — then write the root."
condition: Complete one 5-Why chain per VOD review session. Write the root cause down. After 5 sessions, review your 5 root causes — what pattern do you see?

name: Angle Hold — Noise-Based Width
short: Hold close vs. wide based on enemy sound cues. Click on peripheral entry, then cover.
source: Zasko — "How to ACTUALLY angle hold properly"
why: Most angle-hold deaths happen because the player chose the wrong crosshair position for the situation. Footsteps = enemy is rushing and will wide-swing (hold wide). Silence = enemy is slicing carefully (hold close/tight). Wrong width = enemy appears at wrong position and you miss your first shot. After every shot — hit, miss, or kill — pulling back to cover prevents a trade death and resets you for the next angle.
error: Holding wide by default regardless of enemy sound. Staying exposed after the shot to check the kill feed. Flicking to chase the enemy's head (adds 100-200ms vs. letting them walk into crosshair).
explanation:
Load Kovaak — search "Valorant Angle Hold V2". Set to free play, 10 minutes per side.

STEP 1: Before each rep, decide based on sound: silence = hold close (crosshair at near wall edge). Footstep sound = hold wide (crosshair at far edge).

STEP 2: Relax your eyes on the crosshair dot, not the target. Use peripheral vision to detect when the enemy enters your crosshair.

STEP 3: Click the moment enemy enters peripheral — do NOT flick to them.

STEP 4: Immediately move to cover (press movement key toward cover the same frame you click).

Repeat: correct width read → peripheral click → instant cover.
cue: "Hear footsteps = wide. Hear nothing = tight. Click on timing, then cover immediately."
condition: Zero flick adjustments (crosshair pre-placed, clicked on peripheral entry only). Zero lingering after shot (cover within one frame of clicking). 80%+ correct width reads per session.
location: Kovaak — Valorant Angle Hold V2 scenario, free play mode

name: Shoulder Peek Bait (Left-Side Counter)
short: Bait the enemy shot with a shoulder peek, then wide swing while they reset aim.
source: n0ted — "Shoulder Peek / Wide Swing Method"
why: From a left-side angle, your body is exposed before your gun can aim — giving the enemy a free first shot. The shoulder bait forces the enemy to commit their shot on your shoulder (missing your head), then while their crosshair is moving back to reset, you wide-swing with full exposure — catching them mid-recoil with zero aim on you.
error: Showing too much body on the shoulder peek (more than left shoulder). Hesitating between the shoulder pull-back and the wide swing — the gap must be instant. Wide-swinging first without the shoulder bait setup.
explanation:
STEP 1: Identify a left-side angle (wall on right, enemy to the left).
STEP 2: Strafe right (A key) just enough to expose your LEFT shoulder only — no more than shoulder-width. Hold for 0.2 seconds maximum.
STEP 3: Immediately strafe back left (D key) — pull the shoulder back. Enemy has now committed their shot or moved their aim.
STEP 4: Without stopping, continue strafing left into a full wide swing. Gun already aimed at head height.
STEP 5: Stop on the enemy's position and shoot in one motion.

Chain drill: 5 shoulder baits left, 5 right-side normal peeks, alternate.
cue: "Show shoulder, pull back, then immediately keep going — wide swing in the same motion."
condition: Enemy shot lands on shoulder area (not head) during bait. Your wide swing shot fires within 0.5 seconds of pulling shoulder back. Can execute in Deathmatch without thinking.
location: Custom game — B main / showers / any left-side angle

name: Economy Decision Practice
short: Before each buy phase: announce your buy decision out loud with the reason.
source: w0rthyTV / OD26 — economy coaching
why: Economy decisions made silently in your head go unexamined — you buy out of habit or emotion, not strategy. Vocalizing the decision ("I'm saving because two teammates are saving") forces a brief moment of deliberate reasoning. Over 10+ sessions, patterns of bad decisions become visible: force-buying alone, buying into a bad eco when you should save, spending credits on utility when your team needs rifles.
error: Buying automatically without checking team credits. Force-buying when 2+ teammates are saving. Spending everything on utility while ignoring armor.
explanation:
The drill: during every buy phase, look at your team's credits. Before clicking buy, state out loud (or in your head):

"I am [full buying / half buying / saving / force buying] because [reason]."

Valid reasons:
- Full buy: I have 3900+ and my team is full buying.
- Half buy: We're close to a full buy but not there — Spectre keeps us in the fight.
- Save: 2+ teammates are saving. I protect my economy to reset next round.
- Force buy: We can't afford to lose this round (attacking round 2, or this loss ends the half).

Invalid reasons:
- "I always buy." — Habit, not strategy.
- "I want the Vandal." — Preference, not team-based.
- "I have credits so I'll spend them." — No team context.

After each round: note whether your buy decision helped or hurt the round outcome.
cue: "Buy decision: state it before clicking."
condition: 10 consecutive buy phases with a stated reason that considers team credits. Can explain your economy decision to a teammate if asked. Zero solo force-buys when 2+ teammates are saving.
